texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract We discuss methods to determine L -M -N coordinate systems for current sheet crossings observed by the Magnetospheric Multiscale (MMS) spacecraft mission during ongoing reconnection, where e L is the direction of the reconnecting component of the magnetic field, B , and e N is normal to the magnetopause. We present and test a new hybrid method, with e L estimated as the maximum variance direction of B (MVAB) and e N as the direction of maximum directional derivative of B , and then adjust these directions to be perpendicular. In the best case, only small adjustment is needed. Results from this method, applied to an MMS crossing of the dayside magnetopause at 1305:45 UT on 16 October 2015, are discussed and compared with those from other methods for which e N is obtained by other means. Each of the other evaluations can be combined with e L from MVAB in a generalized hybrid approach to provide an L -M -N system. The quality of the results is judged by eigenvalue ratios, constancy of directions using different data segments and methods, and expected sign and magnitude of the normal component of B . For this event, the hybrid method appears to produce e N accurate to within less than 10 ∘ . We discuss variance analysis using the electric current density, J , or the J × B force, which yield promising results, and minimum Faraday residue analysis and MVAB alone, which can be useful for other events. We also briefly discuss results from our hybrid method and MVAB alone for a few other MMS reconnection events.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Plain Language Summary We discuss methods for determining coordinate systems in order to study magnetic reconnection events at the magnetopause, the boundary between the ionized gas in the region of space dominated by the Earth's magnetic field and the ionized gas coming from the solar wind. We introduce a new method that combines results from multiple methods in order to determine the three coordinate directions in space. We demonstrate this method by applying it to an event observed by the Magnetospheric Multiscale spacecraft on 16 October 2015 and at other times.

texts.orig: Owing in large part to new data from the National Aeronautics and Space Administration's (NASA) Magnetospheric Multiscale (MMS) mission (Burch et al., 2015), a great deal of progress is being made in understanding the processes involved in magnetic reconnection (Burch et al., 2016). MMS has detected thousands of magnetopause crossings (Burch & Phan, 2016), and many papers are being written describing individual reconnection events (see, e.g., the 2016 Geophysical Research Letters special issue, 'First results from NASA's MMS Mission,' and the 2017 Journal of Geophysical Research special collection, 'Magnetosphere Multiscale (MMS) mission results throughout the first primary mission phase').

texts.orig: The simplest description of the magnetic reconnection process is two-dimensional, that is, its structure is confined to a 'reconnection plane.' At the magnetopause, this plane contains the L axis along the direction of the component of the magnetic field, B , that reverses sign (the 'reconnecting field,' positive northward) and, perpendicular to it, the N axis perpendicular to the current sheet (the 'normal component,' positive away from Earth). The M axis, positive westward, completes the right-handed orthogonal triad. In the usual two-dimensional model the invariant direction and the reconnection line (the X line) are along M . An M component of the magnetic field (a guide field) may also be present, along with the all-important reconnection electric field component. However, for the asymmetric conditions typical at the magnetopause, the invariant direction and the X line may be differently oriented (for a brief overview, see Liu et al., 2015). This L -M -N system is similar to, but not identical to, the boundary normal system defined by Russell and Elphic (1979). Our presentation will be directed to magnetopause events but is in large part also applicable to events in the geomagnetic tail and elsewhere.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many mathematical models and computer simulations of reconnection, as well as various methods for the reconstruction of their structure in the reconnection plane from measured data (e.g., Sonnerup et al., 2016), are based on the two-dimensional assumption. For a recent MMS application, see Hasegawa et al. (2017). An accurately determined L -M -N system is indispensable for the interpretation of such events. In particular, a nonzero N component of the magnetic field indicates the presence of reconnection: Its sign indicates the location relative to the reconnection site, and its magnitude relative to the reconnecting magnetic field component,alongwiththemagnitudeofthe M componentoftheelectricfield, gives indications of the reconnection rate. The change of the reconnecting field across the magnetopause, along with the behavior of the guide field, is also an important parameter in helping to establish the overall characteristics and geometry of the reconnection process. To determine spatial scales and perform reconstruction, the motion of the structure is also required. Determining this motion from measured data is a complicated issue that we will discuss elsewhere. (For a recent MMS-based approach, see Denton et al., 2016a.)

texts.orig: It is the main purpose of the present paper to examine in detail a 'hybrid method' to obtain a reliable determination of the L -M -N orientation, as recently proposed by Denton et al. (2016a). It is a combination of the minimum directional derivative (MDD) method developed by Shi et al. (2005), which requires data from the four spacecraft tetrahedron observation platform provided by MMS and the much older, single-spacecraft method called minimum variance analysis of B (MVAB) (Sonnerup & Cahill, 1967; Sonnerup & Scheible, 1998). Acronyms of methods used in this paper are listed in Table 1, along with a description, the relevant eigenvalues and directions obtained, and relevant references, if applicable. MVAB was originally designed to provide a prediction of the N direction, but many years of experience have shown that it can fail to give acceptable results for that direction as a consequence of near degeneracy of the magnetic variance matrix in the M -N plane. On the other hand, the L axis is often well predicted as the direction of maximum variance of the corresponding component of B . For MDD used with B (MDDB), the situation is different: the maximum gradient direction often provides an accurate prediction of the N axis, while there are problems with degeneracy in the L -M plane, that is, the plane tangent to the magnetopause. texts.orig: As a test case, we use one event, namely, an outward bound magnetopause crossing of MMS at around 1306 UT on 16 October 2015, which occurred at coordinates (8.32, 8.50, -0.70) R E in the Geo-Solar Ecliptic (GSE) coordinates used throughout this paper. We examine results from our hybrid method and compare them to those using other methods. This traversal of the magnetopause occurred in the early part of the back-and-forth crossings discussed in the seminal paper by Burch et al. (2016), where it was used to determine their coordinate system. It occurred before the encounter with the electron diffusion region at around 1307 UT (Burch et al., 2016; Denton et al., 2016a, 2016b).

texts.orig: By determining a coordinate system for the current sheet crossing, we do not mean to imply that the relevant physical directions, such as the normal direction across the current sheet, are stationary in time. There are variations, as will be apparent from our results. But our goal is to determine representative directions for the purpose of defining a coordinate system suitable for the study of the reconnection process.

texts.orig: The presentation is organized as follows. In section 2, we summarize the basic data sets to be used and how they were prepared for our analysis. In section 3, we briefly review the methods to be used. Section 4 presents our analysis for the current sheet crossing on 16 October 2015. In section 4.1, we apply our hybrid method using MVAB and MDDB to a 4.5 s time interval, centered at 1305:45 UT, to generate a representative L -M -N coordinate system. These results are compared to those from MVAB using a much longer time period (Burch et al., 2016). In addition, we consider results using other time intervals nested around 1305:45 UT. In section 4.2, we present other determinations of the N direction from: MVAB; MVAJ using the electric current density, J ; MVAJxB using J × B ; and the minimum Faraday residue (MFR) technique using the electric field (Terasawa et al., 1996). Section 5 contains a discussion of how the normal vector orientation from our hybrid methodcomparestothose found from magnetopause models. A summary and discussion of the significance of our results is given in section 6.

texts.orig: The four MMS spacecraft had a nominal separation of about 15 km, with the relative positions thought to be known to an accuracy of about 20 m (T. Williams, private communication, 2017).
texts.text: The four MMS spacecraft had a nominal separation of about 15 km, with the relative positions thought to be known to an accuracy of about 20 m (T. Williams, private communication, 2017).

texts.orig: WeusedMMSFluxGateMagnetometerdata(Russelletal.,2014),ElectricDoubleProbe(EDP)data(Ergunetal., 2016; Lindqvist et al., 2016), and ion and electron bulk velocity moments from the Fast Plasma Instrument (FPI) (Pollock et al., 2016). The data were downloaded at burst mode resolution, 7.8 ms for the magnetic field from FluxGate Magnetometer, 7.3 ms for the electric field from Electric Double Probe, and 150 ms for the ions and 30 ms for the electrons from FPI. All data were converted to the resolution of the electron data (30 ms) using commontimesforallfour spacecraft that were chosen so as to minimize the shift in the individual times of the electron data. The electron and ion velocities were interpolated, and the magnetic field and electric field data were averaged, to the new times using linear weighting. The result was a data set with all the data sampled at the same times with 30 ms resolution.

texts.orig: Some methods that we will discuss below use the electric current density calculated from the particle moments, J = e ( n ion V ion -n electron V electron ) , where e is the proton charge and n s and V s are the density and velocity moments for species s from FPI. (For simplicity, we have simply used the MMS ion and electron fluxes, ignoring the minute presence of He+2 and other species.) We also calculated the current density J curl , from the magnetic field B measuredbyallfourspacecraft using the 'curlometer technique' (Robert et al., 1998), but use of the particle current density led to better results (a larger separation of eigenvalues and more consistent directions; see section 4.2.3).

We also calculated the current density J curl , from the magnetic field B measuredbyallfourspacecraft using the 'curlometer technique' (Robert et al., 1998), but use of the particle current density led to better results (a larger separation of eigenvalues and more consistent directions; see section 4.2.3).

texts.orig: For minimum variance of the magnetic field, Sonnerup and Scheible (1998) state that, as a rough rule of thumb, the eigenvalues should be separated by at least a factor of 10 for the directions to be well determined. However, we emphasize that a ratio above 10 does not always guarantee that the direction obtained is near the desired true direction. Similarly, a ratio below 10 does not necessarily mean that the direction obtained must be far from the true answer. What the eigenvalue ratio does is to provide a simple qualitative measure of the uncertainty of the direction obtained. In this sense, a ratio of 10 or larger is an a priori indication of good quality. In practice, the situation is often complex, which means that, regardless of eigenvalue ratio, the direction obtained must be examined in its geophysical context, including a host of other observations and model expectations.

texts.orig: The accuracy needed will depend on the particular application, but generally, we consider an accuracy of 5 ∘ to be good, an accuracy of 10 ∘ to be adequate for some purposes, and an accuracy of 20 ∘ or more to be poor. Note that, with a total field of say 30 nT, an error of 5.7 ∘ in the normal vector would lead to an error of 3 nT in the normal field component, which is comparable to actual values during reconnection events. In other words, to obtain a reliable value of this field component, an extremely accurate determination of the normal direction is required, which may or may not be achievable in some events.

texts.orig: 3.1. MVA, Variance of a Vector Field
texts.text: 3.1. MVA, Variance of a Vector Field

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For minimum (or maximum) variance of the magnetic field B (MVAB) (Sonnerup & Scheible, 1998, and references therein), the variance matrix M MVAB = M BB is calculated, where M BB ji = ⟨ /u1D6FF B j /u1D6FF B i ⟩ t , /u1D6FF B i = B i -⟨ B i ⟩ t is the i 'th component of the perturbed magnetic field and ⟨⟩ t denotes a time average over an interval of measurements. Although MVAB was originally developed for data from a single spacecraft, it can be used for multiple spacecraft data, either by doing the calculation separately for each spacecraft, by concatenating the data from all spacecraft (as we have done here), or by first averaging the data from the spacecraft at each time. In our events, the results from these different approaches are very similar. The maximum variance eigenvector of M MVAB is expected to be in the direction of the reversing reconnection magnetic field, the L direction. For many magnetic reconnection events, this direction is the most well determined.

So, if the structure is nearly one-dimensional and the two smallest eigenvalues from MVAB are well separated, then the minimum variance direction would usually correspond closely to e N . For an exception see (Sonnerup et al., 2008, page 11).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In some cases, current sheet crossings are close to tangential discontinuities, with only a small normal component of B . In such a case, one can use the constrained minimum variance of B method (MVABC) (Sonnerup & Scheible, 1998) in order to get an approximate normal direction. In this method, the direction along the average magnetic field is projected out of the matrix. A modified matrix M MVABC = P ⋅ M BB ⋅ P is constructed, where P ji = /u1D6FF ji -b j b i , /u1D6FF ji is the Kronecker delta and b ≡ ⟨ B ⟩ t ∕ ⟨| B |⟩ t . The minimum eigenvalue of M MVABC will be zero, with the corresponding eigenvector in the direction of the average magnetic field. The eigenvector of the intermediate eigenvalue is the constrained minimum variance direction. The average of the magnetic field component in this direction is precisely zero; so one disadvantage of this method is that it provides no information about the sign and magnitude of the magnetic field component in this direction (as well as in the maximum variance direction).

Since J is essentially perpendicular to the normal direction and since B N is small in typical reconnection events, by far the largest component of J × B is in the direction normal to the reconnecting current layer. texts.orig: 3.2. MDD, Directional Derivative of a Vector Field
texts.text: 3.2. MDD, Directional Derivative of a Vector Field

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Minimum(or maximum) directional derivative of B (MDDB) analysis (Denton et al., 2010, 2012; Shi et al., 2005) requires four spacecraft to calculate the directional derivative, or gradient, of B . One starts with the matrix /u1D715 j B i that is the j 'th component of the gradient of B i . This matrix is multiplied by its transpose in order to produce a symmetric matrix, M MDDB , which is then diagonalized to find its eigenvalues and eigenvectors. For many magnetic reconnection events, the maximum gradient direction, corresponding to e N , is the best determined eigenvector because of the large gradient across the current sheet.

texts.orig: In principle, MDDB can give the directions at each point in time, since the gradient of B can be calculated using the instantaneous magnetic field. We will use the instantaneous direction to get an estimate of the statistical error (due to different measurements and time variation) for the one event that we will analyze in detail. But for the purposes of calculating a single direction for an event, we will sum the point by point matrices from MDDB into one total matrix before finding the eigenvalues and eigenvectors (Denton et al., 2010; Denton et al., 2012). (Another approach is to use the median or average of the time-dependent eigenvectors Denton et al., 2010.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Burch et al. (2016) analyzed a reconnection event observed by MMS on 16 October 2015 at 1307:02 UT. To determine the coordinate system, however, they used an earlier interval that had a more complete crossing of the magnetopause, 1305:40-1306:09, shown in Figure 1. (Since there continued to be a significant gradient of B within the magnetosheath, this magnetopause crossing may also not be complete.) Using MVAB with the full interval shown in the figure, Burch et al. (2016) found that e L = ( 0 . 3665 , -0 . 1201 , 0 . 9226 ) , e M = ( 0 . 5694 , -0 . 7553 , -0 . 3245 ) , and e N = ( 0 . 7358 , 0 . 6443 , -0 . 2084 ) . Figure 1a shows the L , M , and N components of B av using this coordinate system for the entire time interval with time measured from 1305 UT. Here and elsewhere, the subscript 'av' indicates an average over the four spacecraft.

A better eigenvalue separation for both MVAB and MDDB can be found by picking a time interval that includes just the region of the largest gradient in the magnetic field. But it does include most of the current sheet crossing, including all of the current sheet directly adjoining the magnetosphere, which is less likely to be distorted because the plasma beta is lower in the magnetosphere. And it provides a suitable test case for our hybrid method and its comparison with other methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1c shows the maximum gradient direction from MDDB, determined at each time. For most of the time interval between the green vertical lines, this direction is roughly consistent, albeit with some brief significant variations, particularly just after 47 s. (At that time, the motion of the spacecraft in the normal direction is stagnant and there is some significant time variation in the relative spacecraft magnetic field values; see Figure S1.) But it must be kept in mind that, with a cadence of 30 ms, MDDB provides nearly instantaneous estimates of the normal direction, which on short time scales can undergo large variations caused by local fluctuations. By contrast, most standard single-spacecraft methods give a single estimate for the time interval used, in this case 4.5 s. In order to get an estimate of the N direction for the entire time interval, we will use the normal direction from the MDDB matrix averaged over this interval. The resulting MDDB maximum gradient eigenvector forms an angle of 87.1 ∘ with the MVAB maximum variance eigenvector from the same interval, so the two directions are nearly orthogonal. We will use the MVAB maximum variance eigenvector and the MDDB maximum gradient eigenvector to construct our L -M -N system; for brevity, they will be referred to as MVABmax and MDDBmax.

texts.orig: Wenowdenote MVABmax by e L ′ and MDDBmax by e N ′ and then construct our positive M direction as being along e N ′ × E L ′ . Rather than making e L exactly equal to e L ′ or e N exactly equal to e N ′ , we implement an intermediate choice by rotating both vectors around e M . The angles of rotation are chosen so that if the eigenvalue ratio /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ /u1D706 MVAB , 2 is larger than /u1D706 MDDB , 1 ∕ /u1D706 MDDB , 2 , the rotation of e L ′ to e L will be less than that of e N ′ to e N . Ontheother hand, if the eigenvalue ratio /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ /u1D706 MVAB , 2 is smaller than /u1D706 MDDB , 1 ∕ /u1D706 MDDB , 2 , the rotation of e L ′ to e L will be greater than that of e N ′ to e N . The procedure is described in Appendix A. In the case of our 4.5 s time interval, /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ /u1D706 MVAB , 2 = 73, which is very large; so e L is very close to e L ′ . The result is e L = (0.3894, -0.2938, 0.8730), e M = (-0.0079, -0.9488, -0.3157), and e N = (0.9210, 0.1160, -0.3718) in GSE. More details are given in Text S1 of the supporting information.

texts.orig: Since our method for obtaining the L -M -N system combines results from MVAB and MDDB, we call it a hybrid method. The resulting L , M , and N directions for this current sheet crossing at 1305:45 deviate by 10 ∘ , 35 ∘ , and 34 ∘ , respectively, from those used by Burch et al. (2016), a large difference. Figure 1a shows L , M , and N components of B av for the 16 October 2015 event using the Burch et al. (2016) coordinate system from MVAB for the entire interval, while Figure 1b gives those components along our hybrid coordinates derived using the 4.5 s interval. The L components in the two panels are similar, but the M and N components are significantly different. In the 4.5 s interval between the green lines, the B N component in Figure 1b is approximately constant as expected in a one-dimensional structure. This behavior occurs even though MDDB does not involve explicit minimization of the variance of B N . On the other hand, in Figure 1a this variance was minimized (via MVAB) but now over the full interval. The resulting variance of B N in Figure 1a between the green lines and elsewhere is substantial, indicating that some aspect of the assumption of a time-independent, one-dimensional structure of fixed orientation must be violated.

texts.orig: From now on, the labels L , M , and N will usually indicate the directions calculated using our hybrid scheme for the 4.5 s interval. (Occasionally they may also be used in their generic sense.) Below we will consider coordinate directions calculated using varying time intervals with differing methods to find the N direction. The interval-dependent and method-dependent coordinate labels will be L ∗ , M ∗ , and N ∗ . As was the case for our hybrid method using MVABmax and MDDBmax, some adjustment of the raw directions may be necessary to make e L ∗ and e N ∗ orthogonal to each other. The label associated with the unadjusted MVABmax eigenvector (used throughout the paper as the starting point for calculating the L direction) will be L ′ , while the label associated with the unadjusted direction used to get the N direction will be N ′ . If the MVAB minimum variance (MVABmin) direction is used for e N ′ , then e N ∗ = E N ′ because e N ′ is already orthogonal to e L ′ . But for other methods, adjustment of both e L ′ and e N ′ , as described in Appendix A, is required. Then e L ∗ will not be exactly equal to e L ′ , and e N ∗ will not be exactly equal to e N ′ . Wesummarize our notation for directions in Table 2.

texts.orig: Figure 2 shows results of MVAB using a nested set of time intervals of length Δ t range centered on 1305:45.75 UT. The default time interval Δ t 0 is 4.5 s, the small interval that we used in section 4.1.1. Figure 2a shows the maximum, intermediate, and minimum eigenvalues of M MVAB . The black, blue, and green curves in Figure 2b are the direction cosines to the X , Y , and Z GSE coordinate axes, respectively, for the MVABmax eigenvector, e MVABmax . We expect that the MVABmax eigenvector should correspond closely to the L direction. Therefore, in Figure 2b we also show as horizontal dotted lines the direction cosines for the L direction from the hybrid method using the 4.5 s interval (hence, the notation ' e L dotted' next to the panel label '(b)'). The intermediate and minimum eigenvalue eigenvectors might correspond to the M and N directions, respectively. So in Figure 2c, we show the direction cosines to the intermediate eigenvalue eigenvector (curves) along with direction cosines to our M direction (dotted horizontal lines), and in Figure 2d we show the direction cosines to the MVABmin eigenvector (curves) along with direction cosines to our N direction (dotted horizontal lines).

texts.orig: Only the MVABmax eigenvector is relatively constant across different time intervals. (Compare the constancy of the curves in Figure 2b to the irregularity of the curves in Figures 2c and 2d.) In other words, there is a large rotation of the intermediate and minimum variance axes around the maximum variance axis as Δ t range is varied. The detailed physical reason for this rotation is not clear, but it is related to the fact that the separation of the two smallest eigenvalues is poor (Figure 2a). There is a much larger separation between the maximum and intermediate eigenvalues, indicating that the maximum variance direction is the one best determined. In Figure 2 and subsequent similar figures, panels with gray background indicate eigenvector directions that are poorly determined or less useful because they cannot be easily interpreted.

texts.orig: To examine the stability of the MDDB results using different time intervals, we plot in Figure 3 the eigenvalues and eigenvectors for MDDB using a format similar to that used for MVAB in Figure 2. As was the case for the MVABmax eigenvector in Figure 2b, the MDDBmax eigenvector in Figure 3d is nearly constant over a large range of time intervals. The blue curve in Figure 3e shows /u1D703 MDDBmax-MVABmax , the angle between the MDDBmax eigenvector and the MVABmax eigenvector. This angle is close to 90 ∘ , indicating near orthogonality, except for the smallest time intervals for which /u1D703 MDDBmax-MVABmax drops to as low as 76 ∘ . The red curve in Figure 2e shows /u1D703 MDDBmaxN , the angle between the MDDBmax eigenvector and our N direction. For Δ t range ∕Δ t 0 up to 2, /u1D703 MDDBmaxN is within 5.8 ∘ of zero. Thus, again, an uncertainty of about 5 ∘ for a representative normal direction seems reasonable as long as the interval is not too big.

texts.orig: Thevariations in the normal field components, B N (red) and B N ∗ * (blue), are shown in Figure 3h. The two curves are in good agreement in the nest range 0 . 5 < Δ t range ∕Δ t 0 < 2, which includes a change of sign of the normal field component for the larger nest sizes. The positive values for the shorter nests are consistent with the direction of the plasma ejection jet (see Figure 1f) provided that the reconnection X point was located northward (at a larger L value) of the MMS spacecraft, which were at Z ∼ -0 . 7 R E . Because B L is positive in the magnetosphere and negative in the magnetosheath, B N is expected to be positive in order for the magnetosphere magnetic field to connect to the magnetosheath magnetic field in this outflow region. The values of B N and B N ∗ are both positive at the intermediate time intervals. Large time intervals may include data for time periods for which the N direction has rotated or where more complicated structures are included, and the small time intervals may contain insufficient information to provide meaningful results. The issue of negative B N is discussed further in section 6.

texts.orig: Both the maximum eigenvalue direction for MVAB in Figure 2 and the maximum gradient direction for MDDB in Figure 3 are roughly constant for time intervals up to about Δ t range ∕Δ t 0 = 2. The maximum gradient direction is most orthogonal to the maximum variance direction, indicating consistency between the two methods, for time intervals greater than Δ t range ∕Δ t 0 = 0 . 5 (blue curve in Figure 3e). Values of B N ∗ using our hybrid method (blue curve in Figure 3h) are small and positive, as expected for this reconnection event, for Δ t range ∕Δ t 0 ranging between about 0.3 and 1.25. Time intervals in the larger half of this range are preferred, since they better represent the main part of the current sheet crossing and because they lead to lower statistical error. Based on these results, our choice of the default time interval Δ t 0 (the 4.5 s interval for which Δ t range ∕Δ t 0 = 1) appears to be good. This interval satisfies the three criteria we mentioned in section 2: large eigenvalue ratios, consistency of directions over a range of time intervals, and, at least to a certain extent, consistency of method as indicated by the rough orthogonality between e L ′ and e N ′ . In the next section, we will show how results from other methods generally also support our L -M -N coordinate system.

texts.orig: As in many previous magnetopause studies, our analysis leads to a very small normal component of the magnetic field. This result suggests that the constrained magnetic variance method, MVABC (see section 3.1), could, on its own, provide an accurate determination of the normal direction for comparison with e N , the normal from our hybrid model. The price paid is that MVABC provides no information about the actual sign and magnitude of the normal magnetic field component.

texts.orig: We mentioned briefly in section 2 that use of J calculated from the particle moments led to more consistent results than use of J curl calculated from the magnetic field data. This point is illustrated in Figure 6. Figure 6a is the same as Figure 5d, showing the minimum variance direction of J . Figure 5b shows the minimumvariancedirection of J curl , which clearly is not as constant as that of J (Figure 6a) with respect to the interval used. We do not know why this is. There are better statistics using J , since J can be calculated for each spacecraft, whereas J curl must be calculated from all the spacecraft data. Another possible reason relates to possible inaccuracy in the calculation of J curl due to nonuniformity of gradients.

texts.orig: Table 3 examines numerically the consistency of the different directions that we have used to get e N ′ for time intervals Δ t range ∕Δ t 0 = 0.5 to 2 (seven intervals). While consistency with respect to the time interval is not necessarily a direct indicator of accuracy, lack of consistency is definitely an indicator of uncertainty. The left column lists the various methods that we have examined. The middle column shows the mean value, averaging over the different intervals, of the angle of deviation between the interval-dependent e N ′ and the direction of e N ′ averaged over the different time intervals. Ultimately, the more important direction may be e N ∗ , found through combination of e N ′ from the individual method and e L ′ from the MVABmax eigenvector. Thelast column in Table 3 shows the mean angle between the interval-dependent e N ∗ andthedirection of e N ∗ averaged over the different time intervals. These measures of consistency are best (smallest deviation from the mean) for MDDBmax, MVAJmin, and MVAJxBmax.

texts.orig: This result led us to consider the Lin et al. (2010) model, which is not symmetric around the Earth-Sun line and includes the effective dipole tilt. In this case, we calculated the magnetopause normal direction using the angular position of the spacecraft position vector. The Lin et al. (2010) normal direction was (0.839, 0.495, 0.228). This is 42 ∘ off from the normal direction from the hybrid method. So using the asymmetric model did not improve the agreement. The conclusion is that the magnetopause models do not very well represent the observed current sheet orientation. This is evidence for a large local deformation of the magnetopause, such as could result from a 'lower-hybrid drift-like instability' (Ergun et al., 2017; Le et al., 2017; Price et al., 2017), the Kelvin-Helmholtz instability or a plasma/field structure from the solar wind impinging on the magnetopause.

texts.text: 6. Summary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our goal in this paper has been to determine a coordinate system, L -M -N , for reconnection events observed by the MMS spacecraft at the magnetopause or elsewhere. At the magnetopause, the L axis (northward) is defined to be along the reconnecting component of the magnetic field, the N axis (sunward) is along the direction normal to the reconnecting current sheet, and the M axis (westward) completes the right-handed orthogonal system.
texts.text: Our goal in this paper has been to determine a coordinate system, L -M -N , for reconnection events observed by the MMS spacecraft at the magnetopause or elsewhere. At the magnetopause, the L axis (northward) is defined to be along the reconnecting component of the magnetic field, the N axis (sunward) is along the direction normal to the reconnecting current sheet, and the M axis (westward) completes the right-handed orthogonal system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the past, when the data available mostly came from a single spacecraft, the most common approach was to use variance analysis of the magnetic field (MVAB), with L , M , and N corresponding to the directions of maximum, intermediate, and minimum variance, respectively. To be successful, this method requires a data interval that contains a substantial portion of the current sheet traversal. It often provides a reliable L direction because the maximum and intermediate eigenvalues of the variance matrix are often extremely well separated. But it can give questionable results for M and N whenthe two smallest eigenvalues of the variance matrix are not well separated, which is often the case.
texts.text: In the past, when the data available mostly came from a single spacecraft, the most common approach was to use variance analysis of the magnetic field (MVAB), with L , M , and N corresponding to the directions of maximum, intermediate, and minimum variance, respectively. To be successful, this method requires a data interval that contains a substantial portion of the current sheet traversal. It often provides a reliable L direction because the maximum and intermediate eigenvalues of the variance matrix are often extremely well separated. But it can give questionable results for M and N whenthe two smallest eigenvalues of the variance matrix are not well separated, which is often the case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recent MMS mission, with its four, very closely spaced observation platforms, has opened up an array of new possibilities to circumvent this problem, especially regarding the determination of N , which can now be predicted (sometimes after small adjustments) as the direction of maximum gradient of the magnetic field, provided that the ratio of maximum to intermediate eigenvalue is sufficiently large. And the gradient can, if necessary, be calculated at the cadence of the magnetic field measurements. This method is referred to as MDDB (section 3.2 and Shi et al., 2005) but the gradient of other quantities can also be used. The 'hybrid' methodwehavefocussedonhereisacombinationofMDDBandMVAB.Ittakesthe M direction to be perpendicular to the plane containing the direction of the maximum gradient of B (MDDBmax), e N ′ , and the direction of maximum variance of B (MVABmax), e L ′ , and then makes usually small rotations of e L ′ and e N ′ around the M axis to form an orthogonal pair, L ∗ and N ∗ (see Appendix A). For the hybrid method using MVABmax and MDDBmax, these adjusted directions become our L and N .

texts.orig: The L -M -N system obtained from this hybrid procedure has been compared to those from a variety of other methods (section 3). Among these is the set of three eigenvectors from MVAB on its own. Included also is a version (MVABC) constrained so that the average normal magnetic field component is zero. Also presented are eigenvectors from variance analysis of the measured electric current density J (MVAJ) and of the electromagnetic force J × B (MVAJxB) and, finally, MFR analysis. After this study was completed, we became aware of another recent study that introduced a method called Local Normal Analysis (LNA) (Rezeau et al., 2018). This new method may also be useful; like MDD, it can provide a point by point evaluation of the normal direction.

texts.orig: The principal event for the study is a magnetopause crossing on 16 October 2015, during the 4.5 s time interval 1305:43.5-1305:48.0 UT, preceding the encounter with the electron diffusion region around 1307:02 UT reported by Burch et al. (2016). The chosen interval proved optimal for our hybrid method to obtain the L -M -N system but not necessarily for all of the other methods, with which we compare our prediction for the all important normal direction N .

texts.orig: All of our determinations of the normal direction in Figure 9a are located in the low part of the diagram, far away from the model normals. We conclude that there must have been a large, spatially and temporally localized, deformation of the magnetopause shape during the event, causing a large deviation of the predicted normal direction from the Shue and Lin models. The physical reason for this behavior remains unclear but is possibly related to localized reconnection activity in combination with a lower-hybrid drift-like instability (Ergun et al., 2017; Le et al., 2017; Price et al., 2017), large-amplitude Kelvin-Helmholtz waves, or a plasma/field structure from the solar wind impinging on the magnetopause.

texts.orig: Since the 4.5 s time interval was not optimal for all of the methods, we also plot in Figure 9b the directions e N ′ (blue, red, and green letters) determined for the different methods, where for each method we use the time interval for which the relevant eigenvalue ratio is the largest. In this case, the determinations of e N ′ , including that of MFR, are clustered closer together, slightly lower and to the left of the black dot (the normal from our hybrid method using the 4.5 s interval). Note that the green and blue curves, now dashed, refer only to the black dot. The other methods have individual such curves (not shown), corresponding to their individual optimal data intervals. Note also that all methods, except for our hybrid normal, now have negative, albeit very small negative normal field components. This result is an indication that interval optimization on the basis of eigenvalue ratios does not always lead to more accurate results, especially when those intervals become very small. Nevertheless, the fact that the narrow cluster of new symbols in Figure 9b is close to the black dot (our optimal hybrid normal) lends support to our conclusion that the hybrid method can give very reliable results. Since MDDB uses the gradient of B , this proximity is also an indication that the orientation of the MMS tetrahedron must be very accurate.

We attempted to choose time intervals for which the slope of all four spacecraft curves was nonzero with roughly the same value over a range of time exceeding the time interval. Timing analysis assumes that the structure is static in the moving frame and totally one-dimensional, assumptions that are certainly not exactly correct. Nevertheless, the seven normals from the timing analysis (gold dots in Figure 9) are all within 10 ∘ of the hybrid method result (black dot in Figure 9), and the average direction from the seven timing analysis normals is only 1.8 ∘ from the hybrid method normal. Based on all these results, we estimate that the uncertainty of the normal direction within the 4.5 s interval is at least several degrees but probably less than 10 ∘ .

texts.orig: Nevertheless, it is also clear that persistent, and probably unavoidable, difficulties remain in obtaining accurate predictions of the size of the normal magnetic field component. As Figure 9b illustrates, even getting its correct sign can be difficult, unless the magnitude is sufficiently large. It appears clear that the normal field component for our event, while presumably positive, must have been small or even very small. An unexplained feature is the fact that our coordinate system leads to negative B N in some of the magnetosheath interval (most of the interval between the rightmost green vertical line and t = 61 s in Figure 1b). The more consistently positive sign of B N in Figure 1a was one reason that coordinate system was chosen for the Burch et al. (2016) study.

texts.orig: But there is evidence of time-dependent changes in the normal direction. Figure 1c indicates that the maximumeigenvector direction (MDDmax) was highly variable in the interval 47-64 s, and timing analysis results in this interval are much more scattered than for the 4.5 s interval (supporting information Text S2, Figures S1 and S2, and Table S2). The reasons for this behavior are not understood. Maybe conditions were sufficiently turbulent, on the scale of the spacecraft separation, so as to render meaningless the interpretation of MDDmax as a magnetopause normal. But there is also evidence in the magnetic field (Figures 1a or 1b) of some kind of larger-scale wave activity during this time interval. It is easily understandable that the more steady behavior between the green lines led to a much better MDDB eigenvalue ratio for this time interval than for the entire one shown in the figure.

Define /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ 2 ≡ /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ /u1D706 MVAB , 2 , /u1D706 MDDB , 1 ∕ 2 ≡ /u1D706 MDDB , 1 ∕ /u1D706 MDDB , 2 , and the direction perpendicular to e N ′ in the plane perpendicular to e M , e L ′′ ≡ E M × E N ′ . To get e N , rotate e N ′ toward e L ′′ an amount d /u1D703 N ′ , N = d /u1D703 MVAB , MDDB /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ 2 ∕( /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ 2 + /u1D706 MDDB , 1 ∕ 2 ) . That is, e N = cos ( d /u1D703 N ′ , N ) E N ′ + sin ( d /u1D703 N ′ , N ) E L ′′ . Then complete the coordinate system with e L as e M × E N .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that if /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ 2 ≪ /u1D706 MDDB , 1 ∕ 2 , the rotation of e N ′ toward e L ′′ will be small, yielding e N ≈ E N ′ , but if /u1D706 MVAB , 1 ∕ 2 ≫ /u1D706 MDDB , 1 ∕ 2 , the rotation will be almost the total angle d /u1D703 MVAB , MDDB needed to bring it 90 ∘ off from e L ′ , yielding e L ≈ E L ′ .
